response.justification: Mohammed Al-Said is a capable full-stack engineer with a strong software craftsmanship background, but he fundamentally fails the primary minimum qualification: the role requires 8 years of C++ programming, and his C++ exposure amounts to roughly six months at National Instruments plus a bachelor thesis project — it is not even listed in his skills section. His career is built almost entirely on web and product engineering (TypeScript, Node.js, DLang), with no cloud compute platform ownership, no large-scale infrastructure architecture experience, and no technical leadership in a cloud or DevOps context. The skill-set mismatch is too fundamental to be addressed through an interview.
strengths: Consistent, principled software craftsmanship culture across many roles: TDD, CI/CD, trunk-based development, and pairing practiced at production scale
Exposure to high-throughput distributed systems at Dunnhumby/Sociomantic (millions of HTTP requests per second), demonstrating some performance-critical engineering instincts
Long-running open-source platform ownership (GISCollective, nearly a decade on Kubernetes) showing capacity to own ambiguous, multi-year technical projects
concerns: C++ experience is negligible (~6 months at National Instruments plus a bachelor thesis) against the hard 8-year minimum — the single most disqualifying gap in this application
No cloud compute platform or infrastructure architecture background; AWS CDK usage at Optilyz is product-tooling level, not platform or compute engineering
No formal technical leadership in cloud or infrastructure: mentoring is limited to informal workshops and Code Kata sessions, not staff/lead ownership of an engineering team or platform
